Here's a practical implementation. Email validation has no perfect regex (the full RFC 5322 grammar is enormous and allows things almost no real system accepts), so this targets the addresses you actually encounter while rejecting obvious garbage.

```javascript
function isValidEmail(s) {
  if (typeof s !== 'string') return false;

  // One @, non-empty local + domain, no whitespace, domain has a dot,
  // no leading/trailing/consecutive dots in either part.
  const re = /^[^\s@]+(?:\.[^\s@]+)*@[^\s@.]+(?:\.[^\s@]+)+$/;

  return s.length <= 254 && re.test(s);
}
```

Behavior:

| Input | Result |
|---|---|
| `a@b.com` | ✓ |
| `justin.herrick@netlify.com` | ✓ |
| `a+tag@sub.example.co.uk` | ✓ |
| `a@b` | ✗ (no TLD) |
| `a..b@c.com` | ✗ (consecutive dots) |
| `@b.com` / `a@` | ✗ (empty part) |
| `a b@c.com` | ✗ (whitespace) |

Notes:
- The 254-char cap is the practical max length of an email address.
- This rejects quoted local parts (`"a b"@c.com`) and bare IP-literal domains (`a@[127.0.0.1]`). Both are technically RFC-valid but rare; add them only if your domain needs them.
- For anything user-facing where deliverability matters, regex alone isn't enough. The only real validation is sending a confirmation email. Treat this as a cheap format gate, not proof the address exists.

Want a stricter RFC 5322 version or a looser one (just check for a single `@`)?

```javascript
function slugify(title) {
  return String(title)
    .toLowerCase()
    .normalize("NFKD")              // split accented chars so marks can be stripped
    .replace(/[\u0300-\u036f]/g, "") // remove diacritic marks
    .replace(/[^a-z0-9]+/g, "-")    // any run of non-alphanumerics -> single hyphen
    .replace(/^-+|-+$/g, "");        // trim leading/trailing hyphens
}
```

Behavior:
- `slugify("Hello, World!")` → `"hello-world"`
- `slugify("  Café del Mar  ")` → `"cafe-del-mar"`
- `slugify("C++ & Rust: A Guide")` → `"c-rust-a-guide"`

Notes:
- Collapsing on `[^a-z0-9]+` handles punctuation, spaces, and underscores in one pass, so no double hyphens.
- The `normalize` + diacritic strip is optional. Drop those two lines if your input is ASCII-only and you want non-Latin scripts preserved as-is instead of stripped.

```javascript
function slugify(title) {
  return String(title)
    .toLowerCase()
    .normalize('NFKD')                  // split accented chars so marks can be stripped
    .replace(/[\u0300-\u036f]/g, '')    // drop diacritical marks
    .replace(/[^a-z0-9]+/g, '-')        // any run of non-alphanumerics -> single hyphen
    .replace(/^-+|-+$/g, '');           // trim leading/trailing hyphens
}
```

Collapsing every run of non-alphanumeric characters to one hyphen handles punctuation removal, spaces, and existing separators in a single pass, so you never get doubled hyphens. The trailing trim cleans up edges left by leading/trailing punctuation.

```javascript
slugify("Hello, World!");            // "hello-world"
slugify("  Rails 7.1 — What's New?"); // "rails-7-1-what-s-new"
slugify("Café déjà vu");             // "cafe-deja-vu"
```

Note: `What's` becomes `what-s` since the apostrophe is treated as a separator. If you'd rather get `whats`, strip apostrophes before the main replace: `.replace(/['’]/g, '')`.

```javascript

  return String(title)
    .toLowerCase()
    .replace(/[^a-z0-9]+/g, '-')  // any run of non-alphanumerics becomes a single hyphen
    .replace(/^-+|-+$/g, '');     // trim leading/trailing hyphens
}
```

How it works:
- `toLowerCase()` handles the lowercasing.
- `[^a-z0-9]+` collapses every run of spaces/punctuation into one hyphen, which both removes punctuation and guarantees single separators.
- `^-+|-+$` strips any hyphens left at the ends.

Quick checks:
```javascript
slugify("Hello, World!")           // "hello-world"
slugify("  Trailing & Leading  ")  // "trailing-leading"
slugify("C++ is fun!!!")           // "c-is-fun"
```

One thing to know: non-ASCII letters get stripped (e.g. `"Café"` → `"caf"`). If you need accented characters folded to ASCII instead, add a normalize step before the replaces:

```javascript
    .toLowerCase()
    .replace(/[^a-z0-9]+/g, '-')
    .replace(/^-+|-+$/g, '');
}
```

How it works, top to bottom: lowercase, flatten accents to ASCII, collapse every run of punctuation/whitespace into a single hyphen, then trim stray hyphens off the ends.

```javascript
slugify("Hello, World!")            // "hello-world"
slugify("  Rails & React: a Guide ") // "rails-react-a-guide"
slugify("Café déjà vu")             // "cafe-deja-vu"
slugify("---multiple---hyphens---") // "multiple-hyphens"
```

Note: the `[^a-z0-9]` class drops all non-Latin scripts (e.g. Cyrillic, CJK), so a title in those scripts slugs to `""`. If you need to keep them, swap the third line for a Unicode-aware version: `.replace(/[^\p{L}\p{N}]+/gu, '-')`.

```javascript
function slugify(title) {
  return title
    .toLowerCase()
    .normalize("NFKD").replace(/[\u0300-\u036f]/g, "") // strip accents
    .replace(/[^a-z0-9]+/g, "-")                       // non-alphanumerics → hyphen
    .replace(/^-+|-+$/g, "");                          // trim leading/trailing
}
```

`[^a-z0-9]+` collapses runs of punctuation/whitespace into one hyphen, so no double hyphens. The `normalize` line folds accents (café → cafe); drop it if input is plain ASCII.

```javascript
console.assert(slugify("  Hello, World!! ") === "hello-world");
console.assert(slugify("Café del Mar") === "cafe-del-mar");
console.assert(slugify("--C++ & You--") === "c-you");
```

```python
import csv


def sum_amount(path):
    """Sum the numeric values in the 'amount' column of a CSV with a header row."""
    with open(path, newline="") as f:
        return sum(float(row["amount"]) for row in csv.DictReader(f))
```

Notes:
- `csv.DictReader` uses the header row to key each cell, so `row["amount"]` pulls the right column regardless of position.
- `float()` handles ints and decimals. It raises `ValueError` on a non-numeric or empty cell.

```html
<label for="event-date">Event date</label>
<input type="date" id="event-date" name="event-date" required>
```

That's the whole thing. `type="date"` gives you the native date picker, validation, and mobile-friendly input for free.

If you need to constrain the range or set a default:

```html
<input
  type="date"
  id="event-date"
  name="event-date"
  value="2026-06-19"
  min="2026-06-19"
  max="2026-12-31"
  required
>
```

Notes:
- Value format is always `YYYY-MM-DD` (ISO 8601), regardless of how the browser displays it to the user.
- `min`/`max` enforce the allowed range and block out-of-range dates in the picker.
- Pair it with a `<label>` (the `for`/`id` link) for accessibility.
- If you need date-range selection, time, or fancy formatting beyond what the native control offers, that's when you'd reach for a JS date library or a custom widget. For a single date, native is the least code and the least to break.

Re-renders happen because of how React compares props, not because of anything special about objects.

## Why it happens

On every render of the parent, an inline object literal creates a **brand-new object reference**:

```jsx
function Parent() {
  // new {} every render — different reference each time
  return <Child style={{ color: "red" }} config={{ a: 1 }} />;
}
```

React (and `React.memo`) compares props with `Object.is` (shallow, reference equality). `{} !== {}` even when contents are identical, so the prop is seen as "changed" and `Child` re-renders. Same applies to inline arrays (`[]`) and inline functions (`() => {}`) — all get a fresh reference each render.

Note: a plain (non-memoized) child re-renders whenever its parent does *regardless* of props. The inline-object problem specifically bites when you've tried to optimize with `React.memo`, or when the object feeds a `useEffect`/`useMemo` dependency array (causing effects to re-fire).

## How to prevent it

**1. `useMemo` for objects/arrays** — stabilize the reference:

```jsx
const config = useMemo(() => ({ a: 1 }), []); // deps: recompute only when they change
```

**2. `useCallback` for function props:**

```jsx
const handleClick = useCallback(() => doThing(id), [id]);
```

**3. Hoist truly static values out of the component** — zero hooks, one allocation ever:

```jsx
const STYLE = { color: "red" };
function Parent() {
  return <Child style={STYLE} />;
}
```

**4. Memoize the child** so stable props actually skip the render:

```jsx
const Child = React.memo(function Child({ config }) { /* ... */ });
```

`React.memo` + stable references work as a pair: memo skips the re-render only if the references hold steady.

## Worth a caveat

Don't reach for `useMemo`/`useCallback` reflexively. They have their own cost and clutter, and wrapping a child in `React.memo` does nothing if you keep handing it fresh inline objects. Only optimize where there's a real cost — an expensive child subtree, a dependency array that re-fires effects, or a measured render problem. For a cheap leaf component, the new object every render is harmless.

If you're on **React 19**, the React Compiler auto-memoizes most of this for you, so manual `useMemo`/`useCallback` for reference stability becomes largely unnecessary.

```jsx
}
```


```jsx

```

Database connection pooling is a technique where a fixed set of open database connections is kept alive in a shared "pool" and reused across many requests, instead of opening a brand-new connection for every query and closing it afterward.

**The problem it solves**

Opening a DB connection is expensive. Each one costs:
- A TCP handshake (plus TLS negotiation if encrypted)
- Authentication round-trips
- Backend resource allocation on the DB server (Postgres, for example, forks a process per connection)

If every request does open → query → close, you pay that full setup/teardown cost constantly, and under load you can exhaust the DB's connection limit and start refusing work.

**How a pool helps**

- **Reuse** — connections are created once and handed out, returned, handed out again. The expensive setup amortizes across thousands of queries.
- **Bounded concurrency** — the pool caps the max open connections, protecting the DB from being overwhelmed. Requests beyond the cap wait briefly for a free connection rather than piling new ones onto the server.
- **Lower latency** — a request grabs a ready connection (microseconds) instead of negotiating a new one (often milliseconds).
- **Backpressure** — when the pool is saturated, queuing gives you a natural throttle and clearer failure mode than crashing the database.

**Rough mental model**

```
Without pool:   request → [connect → query → disconnect] → response   (pay setup every time)

With pool:      request → [borrow conn] → query → [return conn] → response
                          └────────── pool of N reusable connections ──────────┘
```

**Things to tune / watch**

- **Pool size** — too small starves your app; too large overwhelms the DB. A common starting heuristic is `connections ≈ ((core_count * 2) + effective_spindle_count)`, then measure.
- **Idle/max lifetime** — recycle connections periodically to avoid stale or leaked ones.
- **Serverless caveat** — many short-lived function instances each holding a pool can blow past DB limits fast. That's why an external pooler (e.g. PgBouncer, or a managed equivalent) in front of the database is common there.

Database connection pooling = keeping a set of pre-opened database connections in a shared cache (the "pool") that your app borrows, uses, and returns, instead of opening a brand-new connection for every query and closing it after.

**Why it helps**

- **Skips connection setup cost.** Opening a DB connection is expensive: TCP handshake, TLS negotiation, auth, session/backend process spinup (Postgres forks a process per connection). Pooling pays that once and reuses it across many requests.
- **Caps concurrency.** Databases have a hard ceiling on simultaneous connections. A pool bounds how many your app holds, so a traffic spike queues for a free connection instead of overwhelming the DB with thousands of connections (each costing memory/CPU).
- **Lower latency under load.** Borrowing an idle connection is near-instant vs. the multi-millisecond (or more) cost of a fresh connect, so per-request overhead drops.
- **Predictable resource use.** Steady connection count protects both app and DB from exhaustion and the cascading failures that follow.

**The basic shape**

```
request → borrow connection from pool → run query → return connection to pool
                         ↑___________ reused, not closed ___________↓
```

**Knobs worth knowing**

- `max` / pool size: too small starves requests, too large overwhelms the DB. Size to DB limits, not app threads.
- idle timeout: closes connections sitting unused.
- max lifetime: recycles connections periodically to avoid stale/leaked server-side state.
- acquire timeout: fail fast when no connection frees up rather than hanging forever.

**One gotcha:** in serverless/autoscaling environments, each instance keeps its own pool, so `instances × pool_size` can blow past the DB's connection limit. That's why you often add an external pooler (e.g. PgBouncer, or a provider's built-in pooler) in front. Pooling helps most for many short-lived queries; it does nothing for slow queries themselves.

Database connection pooling is a technique where a pool of reusable, already-open database connections is kept alive and shared across requests, instead of opening a fresh connection per query and closing it afterward.

**How it works**
- On startup (or on demand), the pool opens N connections to the DB and holds them.
- When code needs the DB, it *borrows* a connection from the pool, runs its work, then *returns* it (the connection stays open for the next borrower).
- If all connections are busy, callers wait for one to free up (or the pool grows, up to a max).

**Why it helps**

✓ **Avoids connection setup cost.** Opening a connection is expensive: TCP handshake, TLS, auth, and server-side session/process allocation. That can be 10–100+ ms per connect. Reusing a warm connection skips all of it.

✓ **Caps concurrency against the DB.** Postgres, MySQL, etc. have hard limits on simultaneous connections, and each one consumes server memory/CPU. A pool with a max size protects the DB from being swamped when traffic spikes (no "too many connections" errors / thrashing).

✓ **Smooths latency and throughput.** Requests reuse warm connections, so per-request overhead drops and tail latency stabilizes under load.

✓ **Backpressure for free.** When the pool is exhausted, new work queues instead of piling unbounded load onto the database, giving you a natural throttle.

**Trade-offs to watch**
- Pool too small → requests queue and stall under load.
- Pool too big → you overwhelm the DB (often `app_instances × pool_size` must stay under the server's max connections — easy to blow past with many serverless instances).
- Stale/broken connections need health checks (validation on borrow, max lifetime/idle timeouts).
- Session state (temp tables, `SET` vars, prepared statements) leaks between borrowers if not reset.

**Rule of thumb for sizing:** start near `connections ≈ (core_count × 2) + effective_spindle_count`; more connections than the DB can run in parallel usually hurts rather than helps.

In serverless/edge environments (lots of short-lived instances), a per-instance pool can exhaust the DB fast, which is why an external pooler (e.g. PgBouncer, or Neon's built-in pooled endpoint) is the common fix.

```
without pool:   request → [open conn] → query → [close conn]   (setup every time)
with pool:      request → borrow conn ─┐
                                       ├─ query ─→ return to pool (reused)

```

Database connection pooling is a technique where a set of open database connections is kept alive and reused across many requests, instead of opening a fresh connection for each query and closing it afterward.

## The problem it solves

Opening a database connection is expensive. Each new connection typically involves:

- A TCP handshake (and a TLS handshake if encrypted)
- Authentication and authorization
- The database server allocating memory, a backend process/thread, and session state

That setup can take tens of milliseconds and consume real server resources. If every web request opens and tears down its own connection, you pay that cost constantly, and a traffic spike can exhaust the database's connection limit and knock it over.

## How a pool works

A pool maintains a bounded collection of already-established connections:

1. Your code "borrows" a connection from the pool (`acquire`/`checkout`).
2. It runs its queries.
3. It "returns" the connection to the pool (`release`) instead of closing it.
4. The next request reuses that same warm connection.

If all connections are in use, new requests wait briefly for one to free up (or time out), which naturally caps concurrent load on the database.

```
without pool:   request → [connect → query → disconnect] → request → [connect → query → disconnect]
with pool:      [connect once] → request → query → request → query → request → query ...
```

## Why it helps

- **Lower latency** — connection setup happens up front, so requests skip the handshake/auth cost.
- **Protects the database** — a max pool size caps concurrent connections, preventing one app from overwhelming the server. This is the main reason serverless/edge setups front Postgres with a pooler like PgBouncer.
- **Higher throughput** — reusing warm connections means more of your CPU/time goes to actual queries.
- **Smoother resource use** — predictable, steady connection count instead of churn.

## Things to watch

- **Pool size tuning** — too small and requests queue up waiting; too large and you overwhelm the DB. Size it against the database's max connections and your real concurrency, not arbitrarily high.
- **Stale connections** — connections can be dropped server-side; good pools validate or recycle them (idle timeout, max lifetime, health check on checkout).
- **Leaks** — borrowing without releasing (e.g. an exception path that skips `release`) starves the pool. Use the language's scoped/`with`-style helpers so return is automatic.
- **Per-connection state** — leftover session settings, temp tables, or open transactions can leak into the next borrower; reset on return.

Database connection pooling is a technique where a set of open database connections is kept alive in a shared "pool" and reused across many requests, instead of opening a fresh connection for every query and closing it afterward.

## The problem it solves

Opening a database connection is expensive. Each new connection typically requires:

- A TCP handshake (plus TLS negotiation if encrypted)
- Authentication and authorization
- Server-side resources: the DB allocates memory and often a backend process/thread per connection

For Postgres specifically, every connection is backed by its own OS process, so connections are a genuinely scarce, heavyweight resource. Do that setup-and-teardown on every single query and you pay the cost thousands of times over, and you risk exhausting the server's connection limit under load.

## How pooling helps

A pool keeps N connections open and hands them out on demand:

```
Request → borrow connection from pool → run query → return connection to pool
                                                      (stays open for next request)
```

Concrete benefits:

- **Lower latency** — reuse skips the handshake/auth setup on the hot path.
- **Bounded resource use** — the pool caps concurrent connections (e.g. max 20), protecting the DB from being overwhelmed by a traffic spike. Requests beyond the cap queue briefly rather than piling new connections onto the server.
- **Throughput** — amortizing connection cost lets the app serve far more queries per second.
- **Stability** — predictable, capped connection count means you don't hit "too many connections" errors.

## Trade-offs / gotchas

- **Pool sizing matters.** Too small → requests queue and stall. Too large → you overwhelm the DB (more connections is not always faster; Postgres often performs best with a relatively small pool plus queuing).
- **Stale connections.** Pools need health checks / max-lifetime settings so they don't hand out connections the DB has already dropped.
- **Session state leakage.** A reused connection may carry leftover state (temp tables, `SET` vars, open transactions). Most pools reset connections on return to avoid this.
- **Serverless caveat.** In serverless/edge environments many short-lived instances each holding a pool can blow past the DB's connection limit. That's why a server-side pooler (e.g. PgBouncer, or Neon's built-in pooler) sitting in front of the database is the common fix.

## Where it lives

Pooling can run in three places, often combined:

1. **In-app** — your ORM/driver pool (HikariCP, `pg.Pool`, ActiveRecord's pool, etc.).
2. **Sidecar/middleware** — PgBouncer, pgpool.
3. **Managed** — the provider runs the pooler for you (Neon, RDS Proxy).

Short version: pooling trades a small amount of memory for keeping connections warm, which buys you lower latency, higher throughput, and protection against exhausting the database.

- **Amortized setup cost** — connections are established once and reused, so requests skip the handshake/auth overhead.
- **Bounded concurrency** — the pool caps how many connections exist (e.g. max 20), protecting the DB from being overwhelmed by spikes. Excess requests queue for a free connection rather than piling new connections onto the server.
- **Lower latency + higher throughput** — checking out an idle connection is near-instant.
- **Lifecycle management** — the pool handles idle timeouts, health checks, and recycling stale/broken connections.

**Mental model**

A request "borrows" a connection from the pool, runs its queries, then "returns" it. The connection stays open for the next borrower.

**Gotchas worth knowing**

- Pool size isn't "bigger = faster." Past the DB's optimal concurrency, more connections cause contention. Size it to the DB, not the app.
- In serverless/many-instance setups, each instance has its own pool, so total connections = instances × pool size. That's why external poolers like PgBouncer exist to multiplex many clients onto few real DB connections.
- Connection state leakage: if you set session-level state (e.g. `SET search_path`) and don't reset it, the next borrower inherits it.

Short version: the optimizer picks an index when it estimates that reading the index plus fetching the matched rows costs less than reading the whole table. It picks a full scan when a query touches a large fraction of rows, when no usable index exists, or when the table is small enough that scanning is cheaper than the index indirection.

## What drives the decision

The query planner uses **cost estimates from table statistics** (row counts, value distribution/histograms, index cardinality). It's not a fixed rule; it's whichever plan has the lower estimated cost.

**Index is favored when:**
- The predicate is **selective** (returns a small slice of the table), e.g. `WHERE id = 42` or a narrow range.
- There's an index whose leading column(s) match the filter, join key, or `ORDER BY`.
- A **covering index** contains every column the query needs, so the engine never touches the table heap at all (index-only scan).
- You only need a few rows back (`LIMIT`) and the index order matches.

**Full table scan is favored when:**
- The query matches a **large fraction of rows** (rough rule of thumb: more than ~5-20% and a scan often wins, because random index lookups cost more than sequential reads).
- **No index** covers the predicate.
- The **table is small** (a few pages), so scanning beats the extra index I/O.
- The index exists but is **unusable** for this query (see below).
- Statistics are **stale/missing**, so the planner misestimates selectivity.

## Common reasons an index gets ignored even though it exists

```sql
-- Function/expression on the column (unless you have a matching expression index)
WHERE LOWER(email) = 'a@b.com'        -- plain index on email won't be used
WHERE created_at::date = '2026-06-19' -- wrap the constant instead, or index the expression

-- Leading wildcard
WHERE name LIKE '%smith'              -- can't use a B-tree; trailing wildcard 'smith%' can

-- Type mismatch / implicit cast on the column
WHERE phone = 12345                   -- phone is varchar -> cast kills index use

-- Not the leading column of a composite index (a, b, c)
WHERE b = 5                           -- index on (a,b,c) not usable; (a) or (a,b) is

-- OR across different columns sometimes defeats a single index
WHERE a = 1 OR b = 2                  -- may scan; UNION of two indexed queries can be faster

-- Low-cardinality column (e.g. boolean) -> planner often prefers a scan
WHERE is_active = true                -- if most rows match
```

## How to see what's actually happening

Use `EXPLAIN` / `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` (Postgres) or the equivalent. Look for `Seq Scan` vs `Index Scan` / `Index Only Scan`, and compare estimated vs actual rows — a big gap means stale stats; run `ANALYZE` (Postgres) / `ANALYZE TABLE` (MySQL).

Short version: the optimizer picks an index when it estimates the index will read fewer pages than scanning the whole table, otherwise it scans.

## The deciding factor: selectivity

The query planner estimates **how many rows a predicate will match** (selectivity) using table statistics (row counts, histograms, distinct-value counts). It then costs each access path and picks the cheapest.

- **High selectivity** (predicate matches a small fraction of rows) → **index**. Walk the B-tree, get a few row pointers, fetch just those.
- **Low selectivity** (predicate matches a large fraction) → **full table scan**. Sequential I/O over all pages beats thousands of random lookups.

The crossover is usually surprisingly low. Once a query touches more than roughly **5–20% of the table**, a scan often wins, because index access does random I/O plus a second hop to fetch the actual row.

## Index is typically used when

- Predicate on an indexed column with a selective filter: `WHERE email = ?`
- Range scan over an indexed column returning few rows: `WHERE created_at > ?` (recent slice of a big table)
- Join key is indexed (index nested-loop join)
- `ORDER BY` / `GROUP BY` can be satisfied by index order, avoiding a sort
- **Covering index**: all selected columns live in the index, so no table fetch at all
- Existence checks: `WHERE ... LIMIT 1`

## Full scan is typically used when

- Predicate matches a large fraction of rows, or there's no `WHERE`
- Table is small (a few pages — scanning is cheaper than B-tree overhead)
- No usable index exists for the predicate
- The column is low-cardinality (e.g. a boolean) so the index isn't selective
- **The predicate isn't sargable** — the indexed column is wrapped in a function or has an implicit type cast:
  - `WHERE YEAR(created_at) = 2026` ✗ → rewrite as `WHERE created_at >= '2026-01-01' AND created_at < '2027-01-01'` ✓
  - `WHERE status_id = '5'` against an integer column (implicit cast)
  - Leading wildcard `LIKE '%foo'`
- Stale/missing statistics make the optimizer misjudge selectivity (fix with `ANALYZE` / update stats)

## Quick mental model

```
cost(index)  ≈ btree_descent + (matched_rows × random_row_fetch)
cost(scan)   ≈ total_pages × sequential_read

use index  when  matched_rows is small relative to the table
use scan   when  matched_rows is large, table is tiny, or no usable/sargable index
```

Random I/O is much more expensive per row than sequential, which is why the tipping point favors scans earlier than people expect.

## Checking it

Use `EXPLAIN` / `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` to see the chosen plan and compare estimated vs actual rows. A big gap between estimated and actual usually means stale stats or a non-sargable predicate, and that's your lever to fix a bad plan.

## Index gets used when

- **High selectivity.** Predicate matches a small fraction of rows (rule of thumb: under ~5-10%). `WHERE email = ?` on a unique-ish column.
- **The predicate is sargable.** The indexed column appears "bare" so the engine can seek the B-tree. `WHERE created_at >= ?` ✓
- **Range scans on indexed columns.** `BETWEEN`, `<`, `>`, `LIKE 'foo%'` (prefix only).
- **`ORDER BY` / `GROUP BY` matches index order.** Engine reads in order, skips a sort.
- **Covering index.** All selected columns live in the index, so it never touches the heap (index-only scan).
- **Join keys are indexed.** Lets the planner use nested-loop / index lookups instead of hashing the whole table.

## Full table scan gets used when

- **Low selectivity.** Query returns a large fraction of rows. Random index lookups + heap fetches cost more than a sequential read, so reading everything sequentially is cheaper.
- **Small table.** A few pages: scanning beats the indirection. Planner often ignores indexes here.
- **No usable index** on the predicate columns.
- **Non-sargable predicate.** Wrapping the column in a function or doing math kills the index:
  ```sql
  -- scan (function on the column)
  WHERE YEAR(created_at) = 2026
  -- seek (rewrite to a range)
  WHERE created_at >= '2026-01-01' AND created_at < '2027-01-01'
  ```
  Same trap: leading wildcard `LIKE '%foo'`, implicit type casts (`WHERE int_col = '5'`), `OR` across different columns.
- **Stale statistics.** Optimizer is cost-based; if row-count/histogram stats are wrong it misestimates selectivity and picks the wrong plan. `ANALYZE` fixes this.
- **Tipping point.** Even with a valid index, once estimated matches cross a threshold the planner switches to a scan on purpose because random I/O per row exceeds one sequential pass.

A SQL engine doesn't follow a fixed rule. It runs a cost-based decision: the query planner estimates the cost of each access path and picks the cheapest. Here's the practical breakdown.

## When it uses an index

- **The predicate is selective** — the WHERE/JOIN condition matches a small fraction of rows (rule of thumb: under ~5-10%). Index seek + row lookups beats reading everything.
- **The column is actually indexed** and the predicate is *sargable* (the index can be searched). `WHERE created_at >= '2026-01-01'` is sargable; `WHERE YEAR(created_at) = 2026` usually isn't, because wrapping the column in a function defeats the index.
- **Covering index** — every column the query needs lives in the index, so the engine never touches the table (index-only scan). Cheap even at higher row counts.
- **ORDER BY / GROUP BY** can be satisfied by the index's sort order, avoiding a separate sort.
- **Join keys** are indexed, enabling nested-loop or merge joins instead of hashing the whole table.

## When it falls back to a full table scan

- **Low selectivity** — the predicate matches a large fraction of rows. Once you're reading most of the table, sequential I/O is faster than millions of random index lookups (each matched index entry costs an extra jump to the heap).
- **No usable index** on the filtered/joined columns.
- **Non-sargable predicates** — `LIKE '%foo'` (leading wildcard), function/expression on the column, implicit type casts (`WHERE varchar_col = 123`).
- **Small tables** — a few pages fit in memory; the planner skips the index because scanning is trivially cheap.
- **Stale or missing statistics** — the planner mis-estimates selectivity and chooses wrong. (Run `ANALYZE` / update stats to fix.)

## The mental model

The cost driver is **random vs sequential I/O**. An index trades a cheap sequential read for many random reads (one per matched row, plus the heap fetch). That trade wins only while the match set stays small. As selectivity drops, the cost lines cross and the scan wins — which is why the same indexed column can be used for a rare value and ignored for a common one.

```sql
SELECT * FROM orders WHERE status = 'pending';

-- "Seq Scan on orders"    → full table scan
```

A SQL engine's choice between an index lookup and a full table scan is a cost decision made by the query planner. It estimates the cost of each access path and picks the cheaper one, based on table statistics (row counts, value distribution / histograms).

## When it uses an index

- **The predicate is selective.** The filter matches a small fraction of rows (rough rule of thumb: under ~5-15% of the table). Few matches means few random I/Os to chase index pointers back to the heap.
- **The column is actually indexed** and the predicate is *sargable* — the indexed column appears bare on one side. `WHERE created_at > $1` can use an index; `WHERE DATE(created_at) = $1` or `WHERE col + 1 = 10` usually cannot, because the function defeats the index (use a functional/expression index or rewrite the predicate).
- **Join columns are indexed** — index nested-loop joins probe the inner table by key.
- **The index covers the query** (a covering / included-columns index). All selected columns live in the index, so the engine skips the heap fetch entirely — very cheap.
- **Ordering / grouping matches the index** — `ORDER BY` or `GROUP BY` on the leading index columns lets it skip a sort.

A query planner picks an index scan when it estimates that reading the index plus fetching the matching rows costs less than reading the whole table. It picks a full table scan when most rows match anyway or no usable index exists.

## Index scan favored when

- **High selectivity** — the predicate matches a small fraction of rows (e.g. `WHERE id = 42`). Rule of thumb: below ~5-10% of the table.
- **There's a usable index** whose leading column(s) match the predicate. A composite index on `(a, b)` helps `WHERE a = ?` and `WHERE a = ? AND b = ?`, but not `WHERE b = ?` alone.
- **The query is covered** — all needed columns live in the index, so the engine skips the table heap entirely (index-only scan).
- **Sorted access helps** — `ORDER BY`, `MIN`/`MAX`, or range bounds can be served straight from the index's order.
- **Joins** on an indexed key (nested-loop join probing the inner table's index).

## Full table scan favored when

- **Low selectivity** — the predicate matches a large share of rows. Random index lookups for most rows cost more than one sequential pass.
- **No usable index** for the predicate, or the column isn't indexed.
- **Index defeated by the query shape:**
  - Function/expression on the column: `WHERE lower(email) = ?` (unless an expression index exists).
  - Leading wildcard: `LIKE '%foo'`.
  - Implicit type cast: comparing an indexed `varchar` to a number.
  - `OR` across columns without a combining index.
- **Small tables** — a few pages are cheaper to scan than to navigate an index.
- **Stale statistics** — the planner misestimates cardinality. Fix with `ANALYZE`/update stats.

## Why "it depends"

The choice is **cost-based**, not rule-based. The planner uses table statistics (row counts, value distribution/histograms) to estimate how many rows a predicate returns, then compares estimated I/O. Sequential reads are cheap per page; index access adds random I/O per matched row. Past a crossover point, sequential wins even when an index exists.

Inspect the actual decision with `EXPLAIN` (or `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` for real vs. estimated rows) — that's the ground truth for any specific query.

## When an index is used

- **Selective predicate** on an indexed column: `WHERE id = 42`, `WHERE email = ?` returns few rows.
- **Range scan** that stays narrow: `WHERE created_at > ?` when it matches a small slice.
- **Leftmost prefix** of a composite index: index on `(a, b)` helps `WHERE a = ?` and `WHERE a = ? AND b = ?`.
- **ORDER BY / GROUP BY** that matches index order (avoids a sort).
- **Covering index**: all selected columns live in the index, so no table fetch at all (index-only scan).
- **Joins**: index on the join key of the inner table.

## When a full table scan happens

- **No index** on the filtered column.
- **Low selectivity**: predicate matches a large share of rows (commonly >~5-20%). Random index lookups + row fetches cost more than one sequential pass.
- **Small table**: cheaper to scan than to bother with the index.
- **Index unusable by the predicate shape**:
  - Function/expression on the column: `WHERE lower(email) = ?` (unless an expression index exists).
  - Implicit type cast: `WHERE int_col = '42'` or comparing mismatched collations.
  - Leading wildcard: `LIKE '%foo'`.
  - Skipping the leftmost column of a composite index.
- **Stale statistics**: planner misestimates selectivity. Fix with `ANALYZE` / update stats.

## Mental model

Index = good for finding a needle. Full scan = good for reading most of the haystack. The optimizer uses table statistics (row counts, value distribution, histograms) to estimate which one wins for a given query.

- **Stats freshness.** The planner uses table statistics (histograms, row counts). Stale stats → wrong cardinality estimate → wrong choice. `ANALYZE` / update stats to fix.
- **Tiny tables.** Few pages → scan wins outright; index adds overhead for nothing.
